content:
First, recall that every known (even) perfect number has the form   
   P(p)=2^{p−1}(2^p−1),   
where p is prime and 2^p−1 is a Mersenne prime.  In particular P(p) is divisible by 8 for every p≥3.

We are looking for positive n with  
   n^n+1 = a perfect number.  

1.  If n is even then n^n is even, so n^n+1 is odd.  No odd perfect numbers are known, and certainly none arise for small n.  
2.  If n is odd then n^n is odd, so n^n+1 is even.  In order to match P(p) with p≥3 it must be divisible by 8.   
    But for an odd n,  
      n^n ≡ n (mod 8)  (since φ(8)=4 and n is odd)  
    so  
      n^n+1 ≡ n+1 (mod 8).  
    Hence n^n+1 is ≡0 mod 8 exactly when n≡7 mod 8.  

So any candidate must be odd and ≡7 mod 8.  We just check small odd n:

n=1: 1^1+1=2 (not perfect)  
n=3: 3^3+1=28 = 2^2·7 = P(3), perfect  
n=5: 5^5+1=3126 (≠496,≠8128,…)  
n=7: 7^7+1=823544 (≠ any small P(p))  
n=15,23,… give either far too small or far too large values to match any known P(p).  

No further coincidences occur.  Therefore the only positive integer n for which n^n+1 is perfect is

\boxed{3}.
